---
title: "CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100"
subtitle: "Ocean, Decolonized"
author: Dr. Hongshan Guo and Class

format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    theme: simple
    width: 1600
    height: 1200
    embed-resources: true
    incremental: true
    center: true

---

# Last Week's Strategy: The Pre-Mortem

---

## Quick Callback

::: {style="font-size: 1.6em; line-height: 1.8;"}
Last week's strategy: **The Pre-Mortem**

*Assume you failed. Now fix it.*

**Anyone try it?** Did you imagine your argument bombing and then strengthen the weak points?
:::

---

## Something You Took Away Last Week

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8;"}
Week 7 showed you **the Doubt Machine** — how doubt is manufactured as a product, not discovered as a fact.

The Hockey Stick Graph attacked. Climategate emails cherry-picked. Exxon's own scientists silenced. Same playbook as tobacco — fund alternative research, amplify dissenters, delay action for decades.

**Epistemic humility:** when someone says "the science isn't settled," ask *who profits from your uncertainty?*
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 30px; font-weight: bold; color: #8e44ad;"}
Now apply that lens to the biggest thing on Earth that most people never think about.
:::

---

## Quick W7 Recap: The Doubt Machine

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.8;"}
Three things from last week you need to hold onto:

**1. "Doubt is our product"** — Brown & Williamson Tobacco, 1969. The same PR firms went from denying cancer to denying climate change.

**2. The Hockey Stick** — a graph so dangerous to fossil fuel profits that a mining executive and an economist were recruited to attack its statistics. Dozens of independent teams have since confirmed it.

**3. The pattern** — you don't need to win the debate. You just need to make people *think there is one.*
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; margin-top: 25px; font-weight: bold; color: #c0392b;"}
Now: what happens when the Doubt Machine makes an entire **planet-scale system** invisible?
:::

---

## From Manufactured Doubt → Invisible Infrastructure

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8;"}
Last week: *"What if the truth itself is contested?"*

This week: *"What if the most important system on Earth is one you can't even see?"*

**The ocean.**

It covers 71% of the planet. It absorbs 30% of our CO₂. It traps 90% of excess heat. And the way we study it — who gets to research it, who profits from it, whose knowledge counts — is shaped by the same power structures you studied last week.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; margin-top: 25px; background: #f0f0f0; padding: 20px; border-radius: 10px;"}
Your toolkit: Spectacle Formula → Complexity → System Boundaries → Timing → Built Environment → Structural Incentives → The Doubt Machine → **now: Invisible Infrastructure.**
:::

---

## Coming Up: Week 9 Preview

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8; background: #2c3e50; color: white; padding: 40px; border-radius: 15px;"}
Next week, we meet the people who are **already drowning.**

The Carteret Islands are sinking. Their residents produce virtually zero carbon. The nations that caused the crisis? They're building sea walls — for themselves.

**Responsibility asymmetry:** the people who suffer most aren't the ones who caused it.
:::

---

# The Story So Far

---

## {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {style="font-size: 1.6em; color: white; text-align: center; line-height: 1.6;"}
We've been building responses to climate change, **one topic after another.**

Before we dive into today — a quick recap of where we've been.
:::

---

## Weeks 1–4

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; line-height: 1.8;"}
| Week | Topic | What You Took Away |
|------|-------|-------------------|
| **W1** | The Spectacle Formula | How to turn a fact into something people **feel** — Fact + Human Story + Stakes |
| **W2** | Complexity | Climate change is a **wicked problem** — no single cause, no single fix |
| **W3** | System Boundaries (LCA) | Where you draw the boundary **changes** who looks responsible |
| **W4** | Timing (The Duck Curve) | The right energy at the **wrong time** is as bad as no energy at all |
:::

---

## Weeks 5–7

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; line-height: 1.8;"}
| Week | Topic | What You Took Away |
|------|-------|-------------------|
| **W5** | Built Environment (UHI) | Every building is a climate decision — concrete traps heat, cities get hotter |
| **W6** | Structural Incentives | Tragedy of the Commons — the system **rewards** destruction, individual guilt is a distraction |
| **W7** | The Doubt Machine | Doubt is a **manufactured product** — same playbook, tobacco to fossil fuels |
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 25px; font-weight: bold; color: #8e44ad; text-align: center;"}
Today — **Week 8: The Ocean.** Let's go.
:::

---

## {background-color="#000000"}

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![A turtle entangled in a ghost net — abandoned fishing gear drifting through the ocean. 46% of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch is fishing nets, not consumer plastic.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/50/Turtle_entangled_in_marine_debris_%28ghost_net%29.jpg){width="80%"}
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center; color: #ccc; margin-top: 20px;"}
*You were told to stop using plastic straws. This is what's actually killing the ocean.*
:::

---

## The Hero Image

::: {style="font-size: 1.6em; line-height: 1.8;"}
That slide just did something to you. Let's name it.

**A hero image is a single photograph that carries the entire argument.**

No bullet points. No data tables. No charts. Just one frame — and your audience does the rest.

This is the Spectacle Formula from Week 1, distilled:

[**Fact**]{style="color: #3498db;"} (46% of ocean trash is fishing nets) + [**Story**]{style="color: #f39c12;"} (a living animal trapped) + [**Stakes**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} (this is happening right now) = an image you can't unsee.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 20px; font-weight: bold; color: #c0392b;"}
Today, watch how we use hero images. Then use one in your own presentation.
:::

---

# This Week's Battlefield

---

## Two Sides. Two Approaches to the Ocean.

::: {style="display: flex; justify-content: space-around; margin-top: 50px;"}
::: {style="text-align: center; width: 45%; background: #27ae60; color: white; padding: 50px; border-radius: 15px;"}
::: {style="font-size: 2.5em; font-weight: bold;"}
PRO-CLIMATE
:::
::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; margin-top: 20px;"}
= The Ocean Is a Climate Machine

= "Protect the carbon sink first"
:::
:::

::: {style="text-align: center; width: 45%; background: #3498db; color: white; padding: 50px; border-radius: 15px;"}
::: {style="font-size: 2.5em; font-weight: bold;"}
PRO-DEVELOPMENT
:::
::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; margin-top: 20px;"}
= The Ocean Is a Resource

= "3 billion people depend on it for protein"
:::
:::
:::

---

## The Core Tension

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8;"}
| [PRO-CLIMATE]{style="color: #27ae60; font-weight: bold;"} | [PRO-DEVELOPMENT]{style="color: #3498db; font-weight: bold;"} |
|-------------|-----------------|
| [Restrict]{style="color: #27ae60;"} industrial fishing to protect carbon sinks | [Scale]{style="color: #3498db;"} sustainable fishing to feed the world |
| [Ban]{style="color: #27ae60;"} bottom trawling — it releases stored CO₂ | [Manage]{style="color: #3498db;"} trawling — banning it kills livelihoods |
| [Integrate]{style="color: #27ae60;"} indigenous knowledge | [Prioritize]{style="color: #3498db;"} peer-reviewed science |
| The ocean is a [climate system]{style="color: #27ae60;"} | The ocean is a [food system]{style="color: #3498db;"} |
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; text-align: center; margin-top: 30px; font-weight: bold;"}
This week: **is the ocean a carbon sink to protect, or a resource to harvest?**
:::

---

## Quick Exercise: What Comes to Mind?

::: {style="font-size: 2em; text-align: center; padding: 50px; background: #0a3d62; color: white; border-radius: 15px;"}
When I say **"ocean and climate change"** —

what keywords come to mind?
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; margin-top: 30px; text-align: center;"}
*Shout them out. Let's collect them.*
:::

---

## What You Probably Said vs. What You Didn't

::: {style="display: flex; justify-content: space-around; margin-top: 30px;"}
::: {style="text-align: left; width: 45%; background: #ecf0f1; padding: 30px; border-radius: 10px;"}
::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; font-weight: bold;"}
Common Answers
:::
::: {style="font-size: 1.2em; line-height: 1.6; margin-top: 15px;"}
- Plastic pollution
- Sea level rise
- Polar bears / ice caps
- Coral bleaching
- Overfishing
- Pollution / oil spills
:::
:::

::: {style="text-align: left; width: 45%; background: #e74c3c; color: white; padding: 30px; border-radius: 10px;"}
::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; font-weight: bold;"}
Rarely Mentioned
:::
::: {style="font-size: 1.2em; line-height: 1.6; margin-top: 15px;"}
- The carbon pump
- Thermohaline circulation
- Phytoplankton (50% of O₂)
- Ocean acidification
- Bottom trawling emissions
- Ocean heat absorption (90%)
:::
:::
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; text-align: center; margin-top: 30px; font-weight: bold; color: #c0392b;"}
The gap between those two columns? That's the invisible infrastructure.
:::

---

## {background-color="#0a3d62"}

::: {style="font-size: 2.5em; color: white; text-align: center; line-height: 1.4; padding: 40px;"}
Let's talk about the single biggest blindspot of climate change.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 3.5em; color: #e74c3c; text-align: center; font-weight: bold; margin-top: 30px;"}
The ocean.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; color: #95a5a6; text-align: center; margin-top: 40px;"}
It absorbs **90%** of excess heat. It absorbs **30%** of our CO₂. It produces **50%** of our oxygen.

And it barely makes the news.
:::

---

## Why "Blindspot"?

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8;"}
Scientists know. The data is overwhelming. Thousands of papers, decades of research.

**The blindspot isn't in science. It's in public discourse and policy.**

- Climate debates focus on **land**: cars, factories, buildings, deforestation
- The Paris Agreement barely mentions the ocean
- The plastic crisis gets attention — but for **straws**, not for the carbon pump
- Ocean acidification — "the evil twin" — gets almost zero airtime

**Why?** Because no one profits from making the ocean visible. There's no fossil fuel lobby for the deep sea. No election won on phytoplankton.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; margin-top: 20px; font-style: italic; color: #7f8c8d; text-align: center;"}
Week 7's lesson: what gets attention is shaped by who profits from visibility.
:::

---

# The Ocean You Don't See

---

## {background-color="#000000"}

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![The Blue Marble — Apollo 17, 1972. This is not a land planet with some water. This is an ocean planet with some land.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/cb/The_Blue_Marble_%28remastered%29.jpg){width="55%"}
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center; color: #ccc; margin-top: 20px;"}
*You live on an ocean planet. You just forgot.*
:::

---

## The Numbers

::: {style="font-size: 2.0em; text-align: center; padding: 40px; background: #0a3d62; color: white; border-radius: 15px;"}
The ocean covers [**71%**]{style="color: #3498db;"} of Earth's surface.

It circulates [**83%**]{style="color: #3498db;"} of global CO₂.

It absorbs [**30%**]{style="color: #3498db;"} of our CO₂ emissions.

It traps [**90%**]{style="color: #3498db;"} of excess greenhouse heat.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.6em; text-align: center; margin-top: 30px;"}
And yet — we've explored less than **20%** of it. We know more about the surface of Mars.
:::

---

## Where Does the Heat Go?

![Change in total heat content of Earth — the ocean absorbs over 93% of excess heat. Ice, land, and atmosphere share the rest.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Change_in_total_heat_content_of_earth.svg/1200px-Change_in_total_heat_content_of_earth.svg.png){width="75%"}

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; text-align: center; margin-top: 10px;"}
The ocean isn't just *near* the problem. **It's absorbing almost all of it.**
:::

---

# The Ocean as Climate Machine

---

## The Global Conveyor Belt

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; line-height: 1.8;"}
The ocean isn't a bathtub. It's a **circulation engine.**

**Thermohaline circulation** — driven by temperature (*thermo*) and salt (*haline*):

- Warm surface water flows from the tropics toward the poles
- At the poles, it cools, becomes denser, and **sinks** to the deep ocean
- Deep water creeps back toward the equator over centuries
- One full cycle takes roughly **1,000 years**

If this conveyor slows down, Europe freezes. If it stops, global climate collapses.
:::

---

## The Conveyor Belt, Visualised

![Thermohaline circulation — red = warm surface currents, blue = cold deep currents. This system distributes heat, regulates weather, and drives nutrient cycles across the planet.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/4c/Thermohaline_Circulation_2.png){width="85%"}

---

## The Biological Carbon Pump

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; line-height: 1.8;"}
The ocean sequesters carbon through a living machine:

**1. Phytoplankton** — microscopic algae at the ocean surface

- They produce [**50% of Earth's oxygen**]{style="color: #3498db;"} — more than all forests combined
- They absorb CO₂ through photosynthesis, just like trees
- When they die, they **sink** — carrying carbon to the deep ocean floor

**2. Fish and whales** — the pump's delivery system

- Fish eat plankton, excrete carbon-rich waste that sinks
- Whales feed deep, defecate at the surface — **fertilising** phytoplankton with iron and nitrogen

**3. The seabed** — the planet's largest carbon vault

- Sediments on the ocean floor store [**billions of tonnes**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} of carbon, accumulated over millennia
:::

---

## The Carbon Pump, Visualised

![The biological carbon pump — CO₂ absorbed at the surface, fixed by phytoplankton, sinks as particulate organic carbon, stored in deep sediments.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3a/Schematic_of_the_biological_carbon_pump.png){width="70%"}

---

## {background-color="#0a3d62"}

::: {style="font-size: 2.5em; text-align: center; padding: 80px; color: white; line-height: 1.6;"}
The ocean is not just *affected* by climate change.

The ocean [**is**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} the climate system.

[Break the ocean, break the climate.]{style="color: #7f8c8d; font-size: 0.6em;"}
:::

---

## Everything This Semester Connects Here {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size: 1.15em; line-height: 1.6;"}
| Week | Concept | How It Shows Up in the Ocean |
|------|---------|------------------------------|
| W1 | **Spectacle Formula** | The hero images today — one turtle, one argument |
| W2 | **Complexity** | The ocean is a complex system — circulation, carbon pump, food webs — not reducible to "just pollution" |
| W3 | **System Boundaries** | Where does plastic's lifecycle end? Oil well → factory → ocean → phytoplankton → your bloodstream |
| W4 | **Timing** | The conveyor belt takes 1,000 years per cycle. Our damage is measured in decades. Timing mismatch. |
| W5 | **Built Environment** | 40% of humanity lives within 100 km of the coast. The built environment meets the ocean. |
| W6 | **Structural Incentives** | The ocean is the ultimate commons. $35B/year in subsidies to overexploit it. Tragedy of the Commons, at sea. |
| W7 | **The Doubt Machine** | "The trawling science is debated" — but the physics of acidification is settled. Know the difference. |
| **W8** | **Invisible Infrastructure** | **All of the above, happening beneath the surface where no one looks.** |
:::

---

# What's Happening to the Machine?

---

## Two Layers of Damage

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.8;"}
The ocean's capacity to regulate climate is being degraded. But we need to be honest about what we know:
:::

::: {style="display: flex; justify-content: space-around; margin-top: 30px;"}
::: {style="text-align: left; width: 45%; background: #c0392b; color: white; padding: 30px; border-radius: 10px;"}
::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; font-weight: bold;"}
Layer 1: Settled Physics
:::
::: {style="font-size: 1.1em; line-height: 1.6; margin-top: 15px;"}
IPCC-grade, measurable, undeniable:

- Warmer water holds **less CO₂** (Henry's Law)
- More CO₂ absorbed → more acidic → **harder to absorb more**
- Warming creates **stratification** — surface layer won't mix with deep water
- The ocean's efficiency as a carbon sink is **measurably declining**
:::
:::

::: {style="text-align: left; width: 45%; background: #e67e22; color: white; padding: 30px; border-radius: 10px;"}
::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; font-weight: bold;"}
Layer 2: Emerging Science
:::
::: {style="font-size: 1.1em; line-height: 1.6; margin-top: 15px;"}
Directionally alarming, still being quantified:

- Bottom trawling may release **~1 Gt CO₂/year** (Sala et al., *Nature* 2021 — debated)
- Microplastics appear to **reduce phytoplankton growth** (lab studies; ocean-scale impact uncertain)
- Whale depletion reduces **phytoplankton fertilisation** (real mechanism; exact carbon impact still being estimated)
:::
:::
:::

---

## Why the Two-Layer Distinction Matters

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8;"}
**Layer 1 alone is already catastrophic.** Even without trawling or plastic, the physics of warming + acidification is measurably reducing the ocean's capacity.

**Layer 2 may be compounding the damage** — but we're still quantifying how much.

This is **epistemic humility from Week 7** in action:

- We don't need to wait for perfect Layer 2 data when Layer 1 is already a crisis
- But we also shouldn't overstate emerging science — that's the Doubt Machine's trick in reverse
- The honest position: *"The physics is certain. The biology is alarming. Both demand action."*
:::

---

# The Five Gyres and Two Islands of Trash

---

## How Ocean Currents Create Garbage Patches

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; line-height: 1.8;"}
The ocean's surface currents form **5 massive circular systems** called **gyres** — driven by wind, the Earth's rotation, and temperature differences.

Think of them as slow-motion whirlpools, each spanning thousands of kilometres:

- **North Pacific Gyre** — between Asia and North America
- **South Pacific Gyre** — the most remote water on Earth
- **North Atlantic Gyre** — between Europe, Africa, and the Americas
- **South Atlantic Gyre** — below the equator
- **Indian Ocean Gyre** — between Africa, Asia, and Australia

Anything that enters a gyre gets **trapped in the centre** — pulled inward by converging currents, with no way out.
:::

---

## The Five Gyres, Mapped

![The 5 major oceanic gyres — circular current systems that trap everything from nutrients to plastic. The two largest garbage patches sit in the North Pacific and North Atlantic gyres.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8a/Oceanic_gyres.png){width="80%"}

---

## Two Trash Patches. Same Mechanism.

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.8;"}
**The Great Pacific Garbage Patch** (North Pacific Gyre)

- Estimated at [**1.6 million km²**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} — **3x the size of France**
- ~80,000 tonnes of plastic trapped and circulating
- **94%** is microplastics — invisible to the naked eye
- **46%** by mass is abandoned fishing nets

**The North Atlantic Garbage Patch** (North Atlantic Gyre)

- Smaller but denser — concentrated between Bermuda and the Azores
- Estimated at hundreds of kilometres across
- Same composition: microplastics, fishing gear, industrial waste
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 20px; background: #f9e79f; padding: 20px; border-radius: 10px;"}
These aren't floating islands you can walk on. They're **plastic soups** — clouds of particles suspended in the water column. You could sail through one and never see it.
:::

---

## {background-color="#0a3d62"}

::: {style="font-size: 3em; text-align: center; padding: 80px; color: white; border-radius: 15px;"}
[**46%**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch

is **fishing nets.**

[Not your plastic straw.]{style="color: #7f8c8d; font-size: 0.6em;"}
:::

---

# Plastic: A Climate Problem, Not Just Pollution

---

## {background-color="#000000"}

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![A blue plastic bag draped over a living fan coral — Wakatobi, Indonesia. Two crises in one frame.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a3/Blue_plastic_on_a_fan_coral%2C_turkey_beach%2C_wakatobi%2C_2018_%2845088490204%29.jpg){width="70%"}
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center; color: #ccc; margin-top: 20px;"}
*The coral took decades to grow. The bag took seconds to drop.*
:::

---

## The Numbers

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.8;"}
- Over **400 million tonnes** of plastic produced each year
- Breaks down into **microplastics** (≤ 5mm) under UV light
  - Found in **human blood**, **placental tissue**, and **breast milk**
  - Biomagnification up the food chain
- Degrades over **hundreds to thousands of years**
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; margin-top: 20px; background: #e74c3c; color: white; padding: 20px; border-radius: 10px; text-align: center;"}
**You are eating, drinking, and breathing plastic. Right now.**
:::

---

## How Plastic Drives Climate Change

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.8;"}
Plastic isn't just polluting the ocean. It connects to climate change at every stage:

**Settled science:**

- Plastic is made from **petroleum and natural gas** — production accounts for [**~3.4%**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} of global greenhouse emissions
- At current growth rates, plastic could consume [**20% of all oil**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} production by 2050
- When plastic degrades under UV light, it releases **methane and ethylene** — potent greenhouse gases

**Emerging research (directionally alarming):**

- Microplastics are ingested by **phytoplankton** — the organisms that produce 50% of Earth's oxygen
- Lab studies suggest microplastics can significantly **reduce phytoplankton growth and photosynthesis**
- If this scales to the real ocean: less phytoplankton → less CO₂ absorbed → more warming
- *We don't yet know the full ocean-scale impact — but the direction is clear*
:::

---

## {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {style="font-size: 2em; color: white; text-align: center; line-height: 1.6;"}
The fossil fuel industry makes the plastic.

The plastic enters the ocean.

The ocean's carbon pump weakens.

The climate gets worse.

[**The industry that caused climate change is also degrading the system that regulates it.**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"}
:::

---

# Overfishing: The Other Climate Problem

---

## How Fishing Connects to Climate Change

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; line-height: 1.8;"}
**Settled science:**

- The ocean's **biological carbon pump** depends on healthy marine food webs — fish and whales transport carbon from the surface to the deep ocean
- **Industrial whaling** reduced whale populations by [**66–90%**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} — whales fertilise phytoplankton through iron-rich faeces at the surface. Fewer whales = less fertilisation = less CO₂ absorbed.

**Emerging research (significant but debated):**

- **Bottom trawling** scrapes the seabed, disturbing carbon-rich sediments accumulated over millennia
- Sala et al. (*Nature*, 2021) estimated this releases [**~1 Gt CO₂/year**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} — comparable to global aviation
- *This figure is debated* — critics argue much of the disturbed carbon may be recaptured by the water column before reaching the atmosphere
- But even conservative estimates put trawling emissions in the **hundreds of millions of tonnes** per year
:::

---

## {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {style="font-size: 2.2em; color: white; text-align: center; line-height: 1.6;"}
Bottom trawling releases an estimated [**0.5–1 Gt CO₂/year.**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"}

Global aviation releases [**~1 Gt CO₂/year.**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"}

One of them is regulated. One of them is **subsidised.**
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; color: #95a5a6; margin-top: 30px; text-align: center;"}
*Governments spend $35 billion per year subsidising fishing fleets. Many would be unprofitable without the subsidy.*
:::

---

# Coral Reefs: The Rainforests of the Sea

---

## {background-color="#000000"}

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![Coral under heat stress turns vivid, fluorescent colours — a last-ditch survival response before full bleaching and death.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/9b/Colorful_Coral_Bleaching.jpg){width="75%"}
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center; color: #ccc; margin-top: 20px;"}
*This is not beauty. This is a death signal.*
:::

---

## Why Reefs Matter

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8; background: #0a3d62; color: white; padding: 40px; border-radius: 15px;"}
Coral reefs protect coastlines from storms, provide jobs, and sustain fisheries.

Over [**0.5 billion people**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} depend on them for food, income, and protection. (NOAA)

Reefs cover less than [**1%**]{style="color: #3498db;"} of the ocean floor — but support [**25%**]{style="color: #3498db;"} of all marine species.

Losing them isn't just an ecological disaster. It's an **economic collapse** for entire coastlines.
:::

---

## The Double Strike

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8;"}
Coral is being hit by **two forces at once:**

**Warming → Bleaching**

- Coral expels the algae that give it colour and food when water temperatures rise
- Without the algae, coral starves. If temperatures don't drop within weeks, it dies.

**Acidification → Dissolution**

- Acidic seawater dissolves **calcium carbonate** — the material coral skeletons are made of
- Coral can't build its skeleton as fast as the water dissolves it

[**Warming bleaches them. Acidification dissolves them. Together, it's a death sentence.**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"}
:::

---

## {background-color="#0a3d62"}

::: {style="font-size: 3em; text-align: center; padding: 80px; color: white; line-height: 1.6;"}
[**50%**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} of the world's coral reefs

have died since [**1950.**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"}

[At current rates, 90% will be gone by 2050.]{style="color: #7f8c8d; font-size: 0.5em;"}
:::

---

## See It: How Dead Is the Great Barrier Reef?

{{< video https://www.youtube.com/embed/BO44JlAElXM width="1600" height="900" >}}

::: {style="font-size: 1.1em; margin-top: 10px; color: #7f8c8d; text-align: center;"}
*Vox (~6 min). "Have we really killed the Great Barrier Reef? No. But we sure are trying." Coral bleaching explained with the discomfort intact.*
:::

---

## {background-color="#000000"}

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![Healthy corals alongside bleaching corals on the same reef — the before and after, side by side.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/60/Coral_reef_with_healthy_corals_and_corals_with_signs_of_bleaching.png){width="80%"}
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center; color: #ccc; margin-top: 20px;"}
*Same reef. Same water. One side is alive. One side is dying. The difference is 2°C.*
:::

---

# Ocean Acidification: The Evil Twin

---

## {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {style="font-size: 2.2em; color: white; text-align: center; line-height: 1.6;"}
Everyone talks about the ocean **warming.**

Almost no one talks about the ocean **dissolving.**
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; color: #e74c3c; margin-top: 30px; text-align: center; font-weight: bold;"}
Ocean acidification is climate change's evil twin — and it's already here.
:::

---

## The Chemistry, Simply

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8;"}
When CO₂ dissolves in seawater, it forms **carbonic acid.**

The ocean's pH has dropped by **0.1 units** since the Industrial Revolution.

That sounds tiny. It's not.

- pH is a logarithmic scale — 0.1 = a [**26% increase**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} in acidity
- Acidic water dissolves **calcium carbonate** — the material shells and coral skeletons are made of
- Shellfish, coral, plankton — the base of the marine food web — are literally dissolving
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 20px; font-weight: bold; color: #c0392b;"}
The ocean absorbed our CO₂ for centuries. Now it's paying the price.
:::

---

## The Evidence: CO₂ Up, pH Down

![Hawaii Carbon Dioxide Time-Series — atmospheric CO₂ (Mauna Loa) rising alongside ocean CO₂ (Aloha station), while ocean pH steadily declines. The "double whammy" graph.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/af/Co2_time_series_aloha_08-09-2023.jpg){width="70%"}

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; text-align: center; margin-top: 10px;"}
As atmospheric CO₂ rises, the ocean absorbs more — and its pH drops in lockstep.
:::

---

## See It: The Evil Twin of Climate Change

{{< video https://www.youtube.com/embed/KJPpJhQxaLw width="1600" height="900" >}}

::: {style="font-size: 1.1em; margin-top: 10px; color: #7f8c8d; text-align: center;"}
*Triona McGrath, TEDx (~9 min). An oceanographer explains how CO₂ is changing the ocean's chemistry — and why it matters for everything that lives in it.*
:::

---

# Who Gets to Study the Ocean?

---

## The Decolonization Question

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.8;"}
**The research gap:**

- Ocean science is dominated by **G7 countries** — the same nations that industrialised first
- Resources and data are extracted from less-resourced waters — often **without fair compensation**
- Western scientific approaches are treated as the only valid lens

**Indigenous knowledge — systematically dismissed:**

- Indigenous peoples have understood marine environments for **millennia** — tidal patterns, breeding cycles, migration routes, reef health
- In the Arctic, **Inuit communities** detected sea ice changes years before satellite monitoring confirmed them
- In the Pacific Islands, **community-managed marine areas** consistently outperform government-managed ones
- This knowledge is disappearing as fishing communities shrink
:::

---

# Let's Bring It Back to HK

---

## {background-color="#000000"}

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![Fishing boats in Aberdeen Harbour, Hong Kong — where centuries of marine knowledge live alongside one of the world's most developed coastlines.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/7b/Aberdeen_Harbour_Fishing_Boats.jpg){width="80%"}
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center; color: #ccc; margin-top: 20px;"}
*This is Hong Kong's relationship with the sea. It's older than the skyline.*
:::

---

## Overfishing in Hong Kong

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.8;"}
**The decline:** Hong Kong's once-rich waters have been dramatically depleted by decades of overfishing.

**The ban:** In 2012, Hong Kong **banned trawling** in its waters — one of the few places in the world to do so. Fish stocks have shown signs of recovery.

**The knowledge gap:** Local fishermen possess **generational knowledge** of sustainable practices, seasonal cycles, and early signs of ecosystem stress — knowledge that is disappearing as fishing communities shrink.

**The climate connection:** Hong Kong's marine ecosystem is part of the South China Sea carbon cycle. Healthy fisheries = healthy carbon pump. Depleted fisheries = broken pump.
:::

---

## HK Trawling Ban: A Case Study

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8;"}
In **2012**, Hong Kong became one of the first places in the world to **ban bottom trawling** in its waters.

**PRO-CLIMATE:** Fish stocks have recovered. Biodiversity is returning. The seabed — Hong Kong's underwater carbon store — is no longer being scraped open.

**PRO-DEVELOPMENT:** Thousands of fishermen lost their livelihoods. Government compensation was inadequate. Many switched to illegal fishing or left the industry entirely.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 20px; font-weight: bold; color: #c0392b;"}
The right policy, with the wrong transition plan. Sound familiar? (Week 6: Tragedy of the Commons.)
:::

---

## This Week's Debate Motion

::: {style="font-size: 1.8em; text-align: center; background: #2c3e50; color: white; padding: 50px; border-radius: 15px;"}
**"This house believes that industrial fishing is a climate crime and should be regulated under the Paris Agreement."**
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; margin-top: 30px; text-align: center;"}
PRO-CLIMATE: Bottom trawling releases as much CO₂ as aviation. Fishing nets are 46% of ocean plastic. This isn't food production — it's climate destruction at industrial scale.

PRO-DEVELOPMENT: 3 billion people depend on fish for protein. Calling fishing a "climate crime" threatens food security for the world's poorest.
:::

---

# Role-Play Activity: Ocean and Climate

---

## Embracing Perspectives

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.8;"}
**What We Are Doing:**

A **role-play debate** on whether industrial fishing should be classified as a climate-damaging activity — within the Hong Kong context.

You will assume **stakeholder positions**: Local Fishermen, Marine Scientists, Policy Makers, Fishing Industry Representatives, Conservation NGOs, Consumer Advocates — or whatever you see fit.

**Why We Are Doing It:**

- To explore the **tension between food security and climate protection**
- To understand how the ocean connects to climate change — not just as a victim, but as an **active climate system**
- To practice building arguments that connect **local issues to global frameworks**
:::

---

## Ground Rules

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8;"}
- This is a [**safe space**]{style="color: #27ae60;"} for exploration and discussion. There are [**no wrong answers**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"}, only opportunities to learn.

- **Respect** and **open-mindedness** are our guiding principles. Every opinion shared contributes to collective learning.

- **Feedback** and **reflection** are encouraged. This is a chance to voice thoughts, ask questions, and grow from the experience.
:::

---

# Your Cheatsheet

---

## Pro-Climate Talking Points {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; line-height: 1.8;"}
**The Ocean Is a Climate System — Treat It Like One:**

- Bottom trawling releases an estimated **0.5–1 Gt CO₂/year** — comparable to global aviation, yet it receives $35B in government subsidies
- Industrial fishing nets make up **46%** of ocean plastic by mass — the biggest contributor to the garbage patches
- Overfishing disrupts the **biological carbon pump** — fewer fish = less carbon reaching the deep ocean
- Hong Kong's trawling ban (2012) shows it can be done — fish stocks have recovered
- Indigenous fishermen's knowledge can guide **sustainable alternatives** that protect both livelihoods and carbon sinks
:::

---

## Pro-Development Talking Points {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; line-height: 1.8;"}
**Food Security Cannot Be Sacrificed:**

- **3 billion people** depend on fish as their primary source of protein — regulation must be gradual
- The trawling CO₂ figure is **debated** — critics argue much disturbed carbon is recaptured before reaching the atmosphere
- Classifying fishing as a "climate crime" will devastate **coastal economies** in developing nations
- **Technology solutions** (selective gear, satellite monitoring, AI stock modelling) can reduce impact without banning industries
- Hong Kong's trawling ban displaced **thousands of workers** — the transition was poorly managed
- The Paris Agreement should focus on the **biggest emitters** (energy, transport), not food production
:::

---

# The Hero Image Lesson

---

## What Just Happened?

::: {style="font-size: 1.6em; line-height: 1.8;"}
Notice what happened across today's slides.

Every section opened with **one image, one caption.** No bullet points. No data. Just a frame that forces your brain to fill in the story.

**Why it works:**

- The brain processes images [**60,000x faster**]{style="color: #e74c3c;"} than text
- Emotion precedes logic — the image lands before the argument
- A hero image doesn't *explain* the problem. It makes the audience **feel** it first, then seek the explanation.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 20px; font-weight: bold; color: #8e44ad;"}
Your challenge: find ONE image for your portfolio that does this. The rest of the slide deck is just commentary.
:::

---

# This Week's Conceptual Takeaway

---

## What You Can't See Still Matters Most

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; line-height: 1.8; background: #0a3d62; color: white; padding: 40px; border-radius: 15px;"}
Before this week, "climate change" might have felt like a *land* problem — cities, farms, factories.

Now you know: the **ocean is the planet's climate machine.**

- The **physics is certain:** warming and acidification are measurably reducing the ocean's capacity as a carbon sink
- The **biology is alarming:** overfishing, plastic, and trawling appear to be compounding the damage
- The **honest position:** we don't need perfect data on every mechanism when the settled science alone is already a crisis
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 30px; font-weight: bold; color: #8e44ad; text-align: center;"}
**Your portable takeaway:** The ocean isn't a victim of climate change. It *is* the climate system. Break the ocean, break the climate.
:::

---

# The Persuasion Playbook | Strategy #7

---

## The Fork

::: {style="font-size: 1.6em; background: #2c3e50; color: white; padding: 40px; border-radius: 10px;"}
*"Are you going to let your family suffer, or are you going to act now?"*

The trick isn't the answer.

**The trick is that both options accept your frame.**
:::

---

## The Science

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.8;"}
This is the **False Dilemma** — or in sales, **The Fork**.

Present two choices that both serve your goal.

*"Do you want the red one or the blue one?"*
(Not: "Do you want to buy a car?")

The brain loves binary choices. Multiple options = paralysis.

**Two options = decision.**
:::

---

## You Just Saw It

::: {style="font-size: 1.6em; line-height: 1.8;"}
When someone said:

*"We can either regulate AI now, or let corporations decide for us."*

Notice what's missing?

**"Do nothing" wasn't an option.** Because they didn't put it there.
:::

---

## Next Week's Challenge

::: {style="font-size: 2em; background: #e74c3c; color: white; padding: 40px; border-radius: 10px; text-align: center;"}
**End with two options — both of which support your case.**

Remove the third option before they think of it.
:::
